skill.name: azure-aigateway
skill.description: Configure Azure API Management as an AI Gateway for Azure OpenAI, AI Foundry models, MCP servers, and API backends. It provides documented patterns for rate limits, token limits, semantic caching, content safety, managed identity, load balancing, OpenAPI import, and MCP exposure.
skill.summary: Configure Azure API Management as an AI Gateway for models, tools, and agents.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, weak-crypto, and jailbreak-keyword patterns, but review found they are documentation examples for Azure API Management, policy snippets, SDK notes, and content-safety configuration. No hidden execution path, data exfiltration behavior, malicious prompt injection, or credential harvesting was found. The residual risk is low because users may copy cloud provisioning commands that affect Azure resources and costs.

low_findings.title: Documented Azure CLI and curl examples require user care
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives for hidden execution. They appear in fenced examples for Azure CLI, package installation, and endpoint testing. The low residual risk is that users may copy commands that create Azure resources, assign roles, or call APIM endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are inside Markdown code fences and are presented as deployment instructions. No file contains code that automatically executes these commands.

low_findings.title: Public documentation and sample URLs are expected network references
low_findings.description: The network findings are false positives for exfiltration. The URLs point to Microsoft Learn, Azure sample repositories, a public Azure REST API specification, and placeholder backend URLs used in import examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs are documentation, sample, or placeholder references. I found no code that sends secrets or user data to an unauthorized endpoint.

low_findings.title: SDK environment access reads an Azure subscription identifier
low_findings.description: The env_access finding is low risk. The Python SDK quick reference reads AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID to construct an Azure management client, which is normal for Azure SDK usage and is not a secret by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is used only as a subscription identifier in a documented SDK client example. No network exfiltration or broad environment dumping was found.

low_findings.title: Jailbreak and weak-crypto keyword hits are documentation terms
low_findings.description: The critical jailbreak keyword and weak-cryptography findings are false positives. The skill discusses detecting jailbreak attempts with Azure content safety and uses terms like SHA or parameter types in normal documentation contexts, not bypass instructions or insecure cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are used to describe protective content-safety policies and skill routing metadata. No prompt injection request, jailbreak procedure, or cryptographic code path was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud resource modification examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes user-run Azure CLI examples that create resource groups, deploy APIM resources, create backends, import APIs, and assign roles. This is expected for the skill, but users should review parameters before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit deployment guidance for Azure infrastructure. They are not hidden behavior, but they can change cloud state and incur cost if copied without review.

content.user_title: Configure Azure AI Gateway with APIM
content.value_statement: AI teams need one gateway layer to control model, tool, and agent traffic. This skill provides Azure API Management patterns for security, observability, caching, rate limits, and MCP exposure.
actual_capabilities: Guides APIM Basicv2 deployment for AI Gateway scenarios.
Provides policy examples for semantic caching, token limits, rate limits, and content safety.
Shows managed identity configuration for Azure OpenAI and AI Foundry backends.
Explains backend setup, load balancing, retry behavior, and token metrics.
Covers OpenAPI import and conversion of APIM APIs into MCP-style endpoints.
Links to SDK quick references for Azure Content Safety and API Management.
limitations: It does not deploy Azure resources automatically without user-run commands.
It assumes the user has Azure CLI access and an authenticated subscription.
It provides templates and examples, not a complete production security review.
Some APIM MCP and AI Gateway features may require specific Azure regions or service versions.

use_cases.title: Protect Azure OpenAI traffic
use_cases.description: Add APIM policies for managed identity, token limits, semantic caching, and model routing.

use_cases.title: Govern AI tool access
use_cases.description: Expose selected APIs as MCP tools with rate limits and subscription controls.

use_cases.title: Standardize AI gateway operations
use_cases.description: Create repeatable APIM patterns for observability, content safety, retries, and backend management.

prompt_templates.title: Start an AI Gateway plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an Azure API Management AI Gateway for one Azure OpenAI deployment. Include prerequisites and safe defaults.

prompt_templates.title: Add rate and token limits
prompt_templates.prompt: Create APIM policy guidance for request rate limits and Azure OpenAI token limits. Use subscription-based counters.

prompt_templates.title: Enable content safety and caching
prompt_templates.prompt: Show how to combine semantic caching, content safety, and token metrics for an AI API behind APIM.

prompt_templates.title: Convert an API to MCP tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through importing an OpenAPI specification into APIM and exposing selected operations as MCP-compatible tools.

output_examples.input: I need to protect an Azure OpenAI endpoint behind a gateway.
output_examples.output: A concise setup plan with APIM SKU guidance, managed identity steps, backend configuration, and recommended policies for limits and monitoring.

output_examples.input: I want lower model costs without losing response quality.
output_examples.output: A semantic caching recommendation with threshold guidance, cache duration choices, and validation steps for cache hits.

output_examples.input: Expose my existing API to agents as tools.
output_examples.output: A workflow to list APIM APIs, select operations, define tool metadata, and add MCP-style endpoints with subscription protection.

best_practices: Use managed identity for backend authentication whenever possible.
Apply rate limits, token limits, and content safety before production traffic.
Review every Azure CLI command and placeholder value before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not paste production keys into policies or command history.
Do not expose every API operation as an MCP tool without access review.
Do not rely on caching or rate limits as the only safety controls.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure resources by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and configuration examples that the user reviews and runs.

faq.question: Which Azure service is the gateway layer?
faq.answer: The gateway layer is Azure API Management configured for AI workloads.

faq.question: Can it help with MCP servers?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for protecting MCP traffic and exposing API operations as MCP-style tools.

faq.question: Does it replace Azure AI Foundry deployment guidance?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on the gateway layer after a model or API backend exists.

faq.question: Can it improve cost control?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers token limits, request limits, semantic caching, and token metrics.

faq.question: What should I verify before using the examples?
faq.answer: Confirm subscription, resource group, region, backend URL, SKU, identity permissions, and security policy thresholds.
